title: [Journey] Building a fresh YouTube channel from scratch
author: hyber
content: Hey there BHW! I recently got an idea for a Youtube channel, or I guess i've had the idea for a long time but i've been just too lazy to start. I've had multiple channels in the past, some of them have made some adsense money, but nothing crazy. Also those channels have died once i've ran out of ideas, and that has been my problem before, but this time I hope it's different, I searched youtube if anyone has done videos the same way in this niche as I have planned, and I found some channels, most of them were completely ass with bad quality and zero effort, but there was one bigger channel that I found that puts some effort into the videos and the channel had like 40k subs and over 10 million total views, which hopefully will become my main competitor soon! So what is my channel about? Im not going to tell the exact method that im doing, atleast not yet, but my channel niche is E-sports related, and these videos can keep getting milked as long as the game is alive. I created the channel and uploaded my first video yesterday, over 20 minutes long, last night it got 30 views and around 2 hours of watch time, nothing too crazy but lets give the channel and algorithm some time to warm up. Also I just uploaded my second video that is around 20 minutes long aswell, let's see if it gets any traction. Still at 0 subscribers but I'm not losing hope yet! ;D My current channel analytics: Research and improvement. I noticed that most of these channels that have tried this method are dead and had barely any views. Those channels also had very bad thumbnails and uninteresting titles, so I will try to improve on those parts the most. I create my base thumbnails with Photopea and improve them with ChatGPT. First I make the base image in Photopea and give Chatgpt a promt to turn the image I made into a flashy and interesting youtube thumbnail with the text that I want. So far it has worked great and im happy, looks a lot like an actual graphics designer made those. I am not the greatest video editor, and the "big channel" that I talked about previously is pretty decent at editing/cutting the videos, but I will try to find a way to get even better. Overall the idea is very simple and the videos don't need a lot of editing but mostly cutting, and thats not a big deal. Recording a video takes roughly around 1 hour and cutting/editing it takes around 30 minutes with my skill. I can easily make thumbnail or two meanwhile my video renders. Plan for the future. So, now I have the channel set and ready to upload stuff, all I have to do is to stay consistent and try to upload as much as possible. I would like to upload a video every day, but since the recording and editing takes around 2 hours a day it might not be possible since I have to work and do other stuff aswell, so my current goal is to upload ATLEAST 4 videos weekly. I have pretty much unlimited video ideas in my head at the moment, and hopefully some of these videos start getting traction. Also I have multiple Youtube shorts ideas aswell, which from my experience can easily pop and get lots of subscribers to the channel. I plan to monetize the channel with youtube adsense, since these videos have zero copyrighted material, and also I will try to monetize with amazon affiliate by linking the e-sport players gear in the description. Thank you if you read this far, let's see if this works out!

title: CTR Manipulaton for SEO - AMA
author: theautomationdomination
content: I've been doing a lot of testing the last 3 months on CTR and referral traffic for ranking. Ask me any questions you have and I'll answer them. The best strategy to get from page 3-10 is to spoof social referral traffic. Once you hit page 1-2 add branded + keyword searches. Once you hit page 1 add in your keyword searches. And once you hit top 3 use a system like push/pull to pogostick the websites above you and send negative traffic signals like high bounce rate (5-25 second visits) before clicking and going to your website and spending 5-10 minutes clicking around on your inner pages. This one I had to outrank a reddit thread. And the only way I was able to do it was with pogosticking the reddit thread. Another thing to be aware of. If it doesn't show up in Google analytics that means it's not working.

title: The 6 Circles of YouTube Automation Hell: A Brutally Honest Reality Check
author: TopOneYouTube
content: Hey BHW, Summer is here, the sun is shining, and every single YouTube "guru" on your feed is selling a course on how easy it is to copy-paste your way to $10k/month with automation. But nobody talks about the actual trenches. Nobody talks about the psychological damage, the loops, and the hidden traps that wait for every beginner. If this post doesn’t kill your desire to start a YouTube channel - congratulations, you actually have the balls for this business. Welcome to the **6 Circles of YouTube Automation Hell**. Let’s dive in. --- ### Circle 1: The Google Account Suspension Trap You buy a fresh domain, set up your emails, or create a brand new Google account. You feel like a businessman. One week later, before you even upload a profile picture: **ACCOUNT SUSPENDED.** *Your emotional state:* Not a big deal. You submit a quick appeal, wait a couple of days, and get it back. You think the worst is behind you. Oh, you sweet summer child. ### Circle 2: The Cortisol Rollercoaster You register the channel. You spend days making your first "masterpiece." You upload it... and **0 views**. Your urge to get rich quickly drops, your cortisol levels spike, and you quietly close the browser tab where you had a BMW M4 configurator open. By video #10, you are questioning your life choices. By video #20, suddenly, one video gets 400 views. Then 4,000. Then it hits the algorithm and explodes to 40k views. You instantly hit the 4,000 watch hours and 1,000 subscribers mark. *The dopamine hit is insane.* The M4 configurator is replaced by an X6M. You feel like the king of SEO. ### Circle 3: The AdSense & Reused Content Nightmare You apply for AdSense. Your heart is pounding. You wake up every 30 minutes to check your phone. You finally fall asleep out of sheer exhaustion. You wake up, open the dashboard, and see: **Application Rejected: Reused Content.** Total epic tragedy. YouTube feels like a scam, and your emotional stability is at absolute zero. You spend the next 48 hours researching how to record a perfect video appeal. You send it. 12 hours later... **APPROVED. Monetization is ON!** ### Circle 4: The "Shadowban" & Copycat Plague The channel is monetized. You keep uploading, expecting the cash to flow. Instead, YouTube completely stops giving you impressions. The channel flatlines at 800 views a day. Hope is fading. To make things worse, competitors have spotted your niche. While you were celebrating, someone else cloned your exact framework and launched a 5-channel network doing the same thing, stealing your potential reach. ### Circle 5: The Breakout (The Illusion of Success) It’s month two. You have 60 videos under your belt. It’s heavy to carry, but you’ve invested too much to quit. Even your friends have stopped clicking your links to give you pity likes. You go to Instagram to cope and look at other successful entrepreneurs, then return to your analytics dashboard, and your jaw drops. *The videos started printing.* Your latest video has 100k views. Your very first video suddenly revived and has 250k. You are pulling 500,000 views a day. With a solid $5 RPM, that’s roughly **$2,500/day**. Life is good. You start looking at real estate. ### Circle 6: The Final Boss You wake up, grab your coffee, refresh your phone to see how much money you made overnight, and get greeted by a clean, red banner: **"Your account has been terminated due to a violation of YouTube's Terms of Service."** The End. --- YouTube Automation is a highly lucrative business, but it is **not** a get-rich-quick scheme. It’s a game of mental stamina, systems, and diversification. If you put all your eggs in one basket and expect a smooth ride, Circle 6 will destroy you. Which circle are you currently stuck in right now? Let’s talk below!

title: Is Email Marketing Still Valuable?
author: guruto luther
content: I've always been curious about something. Affiliate marketing strategies have been proven to be not only feasible but also cost-effective and efficient through market practice. So why do many businesses still insist on email marketing? Personally, I receive dozens of spam emails every day and never care to pay attention to their content. The constant notification sounds from emails only annoy me, and I believe I'm not the only one feeling this way. With this question in mind, I consulted a friend in the industry. He told me that email marketing remains important because it allows businesses to communicate directly with a large audience at a relatively low cost and enables personalized communication with customers, leading to higher engagement and conversion rates. For instance, businesses can use customer data, such as purchase history, browsing behavior, and demographic information, to create more relevant and personalized email campaigns. However, I countered, consumers have a high aversion to spam emails, and your product promotion may not necessarily be effective. He acknowledged this and said that their primary marketing strategy is still affiliate marketing. They manage multiple platform accounts through tools like more login to maintain a basic user base, and email marketing serves more as a supplementary means. I didn’t know what to say to his response; all I know is that I still have to endure the torment of spam emails. I also want to ask everyone, does anyone really make purchases based on the content in emails?

title: [JOURNEY] Growing my webshop revenue with cloaked ads
author: Ronny Jay
content: Hey everyone, After lurking on BHW for over a year, I’m finally sharing my journey. About 6 months ago I started a “sexual health” webshop. The setup is simple: I get the orders in, my supplier handles shipping. I run this solo. Current revenue: about €6–9k/month (SEO only), growing every month First Goal: at least €15k/month within 6 months and scale beyond Market: Only EU (no plans for US or Asia yet) I’ve always focused on SEO. I found longtail keywords in the EU market that bring steady traffic. I’m building out more content using ChatGPT (with humanized prompts) for product page. Just the basic content for now, and then I optimize the text for on-page SEO. It’s a lot of work, but worth it. The biggest headache so far has been payments. I burned through 4 stealth Stripe accounts and high risk merchant accounts that claimed to be no KYC. A few from sellers here on BHW to be honest. But they all got shut down fast, even though I followed all the rules. Lost about €4k that way. I finally found a stable no KYC payment setup that’s been working well so far. Biggest advice: don’t buy Stripe accounts without a proper setup. Find a good partner or maybe a JV. Rookie mistake I guess. Things I’m focusing on right now: 1. Recently I started noticing more Facebook ads that seem to link directly to other pharma/nutra sites. Probably cloaked, now that I think about it. But it opened my eyes. I’ve never touched FB ads before, and I’m sure that’s the piece I’m missing to really scale. I’m reading up on Facebook stealth setups, proxies, warmed accounts, etc. The info is scattered, so if anyone knows a solid guide or megathread that explains it from A to Z, I’d really appreciate it. I’ve been going through the marketplace. I see lots of FB agencies, but no threads on how it actually works with cloaking etc. I just want to avoid the common traps and maybe save some money during testing. The next step will be crucial, and I want to do it right. 2. More focus on AIO. I actually asked ChatGPT some questions (in my native language) and somehow my webshop popped up as an answer . I honestly don’t know exactly how, but I’ll figure it out. Reading and watching more about AIO is one of my goals, next to getting stealth ads up and running. 3. Email marketing. I’ve got a few loyal clients now, but lots of new clients too. I’m trying to figure out how to do email marketing in this business without getting banned. Tried Brevo, but they already banned me. So now I need to learn how to do this the stealth way. Thanks to everyone here who has helped me silently over the past year. I’ve learned more from this forum than anywhere else. I’ll post updates as I test things. If you’re on a similar path, feel free to follow along. I’ll share what works and what doesn’t. I hope I can look back in 6 months and say I’ve reached my first goal.

title: Is high-ticket E-com still viable using mainly blog traffic in 2025?
author: CCIS
content: I’m evaluating whether a high-ticket e-commerce store can still be built in 2025 using blog-driven organic traffic as the primary acquisition channel. My plan is straightforward: • Main growth engine: consistent long-form blog content targeting commercial-investigational queries. • Support methods: AI-assisted drafts, manual refinement, and possibly light programmatic SEO. • Daily commitment: ~5 hours of focused work. • Model: classic high-ticket dropshipping initially, with a shift toward a more hybrid setup after generating stable sales. • Experience: limited SEO/affiliate experience, but previous low-ticket dropshipping background (got a few SEO sales but it was mainly people searching our brand). I’ve researched a direct competitor store that appears to rely almost entirely on organic content. No ads, no social pushes, just steady blog output mostly AI-generated from what I can tell (copied a bunch of their blogs into an ai detector and it always comes positive). Their success raises the question of whether the same strategy is still replicable for a new build. What I want clarity on: Is a content-first approach still a viable path for a high-ticket store in 2025? Does relying on blogging slow down the time-to-first-sale, or can it produce early traction with the right content map? For those who’ve scaled similar models, how much traffic typically precedes the first high-ticket conversions? Are current algorithm conditions (helpful content signals, topical authority requirements, volatility) making organic-first stores harder or easier to launch? For anyone running high-ticket setups today, is organic content still your primary driver or just one component of a broader mix? Any data, case studies, or hard-earned lessons from recent builds in similar niches would be valuable. Note: This post was written with AI assistance; please excuse any mistakes or formatting issues.

title: Black Hat Link Building Service
author: ahrefseo
content: Black Hat Link Building Price & Package ​ Black hat link building is one of the most talked-about and misunderstood topics in SEO. Some marketers treat it like a forbidden shortcut, while others quietly use it to compete in markets where traditional link building simply isn’t powerful enough.​ The truth sits somewhere in the middle.​ Black hat link building refers to link strategies that intentionally bypass or stretch search engine guidelines in order to influence rankings more quickly than white-hat methods. These links are considered high-reward, and they are commonly used in industries where competition is aggressive, timelines are short, or ROI matters more than long-term brand.​ Black hat link building is not for every website. It is not recommended for government sites, large corporate brands, or businesses that depend on long-term reputation. However, when used strategically, with planning and experience, it can deliver measurable results.​ Affiliate marketers, churn-and-burn projects, testing domains, and highly competitive niches rely on black hat links every day — often without publicly admitting it.​ ​What Are Black Hat Links?​​Black hat links are backlinks created using methods that violate or intentionally manipulate search engine guidelines. These links are designed to influence rankings quickly rather than build long-term authority.​​Common examples include:​​ Links from Private Blog Networks (PBNs)​ Paid contextual links placed at scale​ Automated backlinks created through software​ Links generated using expired domains​ Tier-2 and tier-3 spam links pointing to power pages​ ​An important distinction most competitors fail to explain is the difference between intentional and accidental black hat links.​​Intentional black hat links are created deliberately as part of an SEO strategy. Accidental black hat links happen when competitors attack a site with spam links, or when a site unknowingly hires low-quality providers.​​Link Velocity Manipulation (Often Ignored)​​One of the most overlooked aspects of black hat link building is link velocity — how 1st links are created.​Search engines expect backlinks to grow at a natural pace. When a website suddenly gains hundreds or thousands of links overnight, it raises red flags. Skilled operators control link velocity by:​ Spreading link creation over time​ Mixing link types​ Using tiered structures​ Matching link growth to content publishing​ ​Most penalties happen not because links exist, but because they appear unnaturally unbalanced.​​Black Hat SEO Is the Alternative to Gray or White Hat SEO​​Not all link building is equal. The difference between white, gray, and black hat SEO comes down to tolerance, speed, and control.​​White Hat SEO​ ​Slow results​ Limited control over anchors​ High cost​ Suitable for brands and long-term businesses​ ​Gray Hat SEO​ ​Moderate speed​ Partial control​ Often used quietly by agencies​ ​Black Hat SEO​ ​Full control over anchors and URLs​ Lower cost​ Used in aggressive and competitive markets​ ​Experienced teams reduce through controlled execution, proper pacing, and realistic expectations.​​​​Why Do People Build Black Hat Links?​​People turn to black hat link building for one simple reason: competition.​In many niches, white-hat methods alone are not enough. When every competitor is buying links, running networks, or pushing aggressive anchor strategies, staying “clean” often means staying invisible.​​​Other common reasons include:​​ Time pressure to rank quickly​ Algorithm delays for new domains​ Launching short-term campaigns​ Testing offers or markets​ Limited budgets for expensive outreach​ ​Emerging Markets & Local SEO Context​In emerging markets like Indonesia, link ecosystems behave differently. Local directories, expired domains, and regional blogs are often easier to control. Businesses targeting local or regional keywords frequently use aggressive links to establish visibility before competitors catch up.​​Short campaign SEO is another overlooked factor. Product launches, promotions, and affiliate offers do not always need to rank for years. Sometimes ranking for a few months is enough to generate profit.​​​​Why You Need Black Hat Link Building?​ Most competitors avoid answering this directly. The reality is that black hat link building exists because it works in specific scenarios.​ You may need black hat link building if:​ You are targeting highly competitive SERPs You need quick rankings, not in 12 months You are testing new niches or domains You want to support an existing white-hat foundation You operate in aggressive industries like gambling, crypto, or adult This is not about random spam. Strategic black hat SEO is planned, structured, and monitored.​ ​1) Black Hat Link Building Can Have a Short-Term Benefit​ Black hat links are well known for delivering:​ 1st ranking movement Quick traffic growth indexing of new pages Short-term results are often enough for:​ Affiliate campaigns Event pages Offer landi

title: Building a Reddit presence for a D2C brand step by step
author: TheStrategos
content: Week 1-2: Set up account, read subreddit rules, comment only. No posts. Build karma and familiarity. Week 3-4: Start posting value content in 2-3 target subreddits. Month 2: Introduce brand mentions naturally in comments. Use format: 'I faced this problem too, we actually built something for this at [brand]' Month 3: Start an AMA (Ask Me Anything) as the brand founder in a relevant subreddit. This gets massive organic reach. Pro tip: Never rush the timeline. Brands that skip month 1 and 2 get banned. Patience is the competitive advantage most marketers lack. Thanks TheStrategos

title: programmatic display under $2k/month - actually worth it or just burning cash
author: angryPixel
content: like comparing a speedboat to a kiddie pool. everyone wants programmatic but most small advertisers don't realize the minimum spend to exit the learning phase on most DSPs is $1,500-2,000 alone. at that budget you're fighting CPMs of $4-8 on open exchange while your frequency caps are basically useless because you don't have enough reach to matter. seen accounts spend 6 weeks "optimizing" with sample sizes too small to mean anything. private marketplace deals require $5k+ minimums on most quality publishers anyway so at sub-2k you're stuck on open exchange garbage inventory. retargeting can still work if your site does 10k+ visitors/month since you're working a warm audience. prospecting at this budget though, forget it. maybe meta or google pmax actually makes more sense until you scale up. anyone here actually cracked a profitable programmatic setup under $2k or is it always a stepping stone budget?

author: Traag _ KNetWork
content: A few months ago, I launched a small campaign to promote a crypto exchange affiliate offer. Targeted, compliant (at least I thought so), no flashy claims. Just clean value and instructions. Still got flagged. At first, I panicked. Then frustrated. Then curious. Why does this happen even when we try to play by the rules? Click to expand... Turns out, the issue wasn’t in the content — but in the way platforms interpret intent. Crypto, by nature, carries a risk tag. Combine that with affiliate links and you’re on thin ice even before your first impression lands. 3 lessons I’ve learned: Always use link wrappers or mini blogs (Linktree/Notion helps a lot) Avoid spammy phrases like “bonus”, “free”, “get rich”, “claim now” If you're scaling, use backup domains and rotation tools – just in case Now, I treat every campaign like a negotiation: “How can I say what I mean, while passing through the filter first?” Click to expand... How do you guys manage ad compliance for crypto/finance verticals? Would love to hear from anyone who's run long-term campaigns successfully.

title: [Journey] Lots of Cash to $500k/Month & Exit (AI OFM? Content? Figuring It Out As I Go)
author: apexstudio
content: The goal of this is just to keep me accountable and hopefully find some folks I can connect with to help decide a path forwards. Setting the stage: Have sold a few of my companies/digital assets now over the years. Notable for here was selling my OFM company in 2024 and then selling my traffic/OFM backbone company I started a month later just a few months ago. Beginning 2025 I started another company with some partners way outside of that space and am sitting steady at $400-500k/month. But the company no longer needs my time and it has no real growth path beyond that. So I'm sitting on a bunch of capital because I don't like spending money on stupid stuff like lambos or otherwise. Trying to figure out how to best leverage my cash and time. Areas I've been looking into and ones I'll be researching to start this journey from: 1) Car rentals for delivery gig workers (ie. uber eats) - I like this one because I think it can scale up over 12 - 18 months to significant profit and then be sold before autonomous delivery drivers become a common place fear 2) AI OFM but keep it content/traffic focused - love the idea of infinite scale based on automation, but honestly have zero idea how the generation side works and I HATE manual labor in content generation/posting, but I know hiring for all this would be super easy 3) Pure AI content focused and build a media network - I know tiktop shop and amazon affiliate type video content is lucrative and potentially scalable if I can mix in AI to build a self liquidating machine that builds up buyer lists to use for further partnerships and cashflow models I'm liking the idea of a model that has a high capital/time barrier to entry because I can dump money in and hire to supplement right away. But will also research some other biz models - hence the weird title. It just has to be scalable and saleable so I can get it to exit. Stage one of this journey is for me to figure out all the options on the table where: 1) I can leverage $200-500k in capital to build up infrastructure rapidly 2) I can have leverage over time and hire within reason (no crazy complex elements where my hiring pool is 1000 people world wide) 3) There's scalability to $400-500k/month with low barriers (ie. youtube getting to this marker is hard, there's 1000s who've got there high end - it's a small rate of success to get there compared to global population) 4) There's ability to exit to some degree at a 2+ year run rate of cashflows - aiming to balance this against leverage of time 5) Competition and barrier to entry is somewhere in the middle, but not on the edge of hyper saturation - it's fine if $200k in resources have to go into getting to the first $1,000 back because that means saturation of the business will be low This will be a weird one but I'll share everything going on as I go. My first goal is to get on researching and ChatGPT to get 10-15 potential models that can work, rate them based on the 5 criteria above. I'll share after I get done, detail will depend on what I find. Same with the last journey - any and all insight or ideas is always welcome!

content: Hi everyone, I’m dealing with a copyright issue and I’m looking for advice from people who have experience with offshore hosting providers. There is a website hosting my copyrighted video content without permission. The site appears to be hosted by **KoDDoS** and is using **Cloudflare** as a proxy/CDN. Here is what I have already done: • Sent multiple DMCA notices to the hosting provider (KoDDoS) • Opened support tickets with them, but they were closed without any response • Contacted the domain registrar (**Namecheap**) • Reported the domain to **Cloudflare** through their abuse form • Submitted copyright removal requests to **Google** Despite all of this, the content is still online on the hosting server. My question is: Has anyone here successfully removed copyrighted content from offshore hosts like KoDDoS that tend to ignore DMCA requests? If the hosting provider refuses to act, what are the most effective alternatives? For example: • contacting upstream providers • targeting the domain registrar • getting the CDN removed • other strategies that actually work in these situations Any advice or experience would be really appreciated. Thanks!

title: No more FAQ rich results

title: Suggestion: Flagging users, posts and comments as AI on BlackHatWorld
author: Venusaur
content: Ok, I got it. BHW is not entirely against AI content and I agree, because there are always that 5% of people using it for a good purpose. But most users are not well-intended, they just want to flood, inflate their post counter and generate useless thread to this forum. With that in mind, I thought about flagging this kind of content, user and comments as AI-generated, so we don't need to waste our time reading it or dealing with botted users. 01) AI Posts - a mod can put this tag/flag indicating that the post will remain active and it's not against BHW rules, but it is AI generated, so, if you want to waste your time reading generic info, go ahead. After some reports from members, mods can detect those posts. My additional suggestion: a post flagged as AI generated doesn't count for your post counter - that would discourage them. Example: 02) AI Users - after several reports, I guess a member can receive this badge. Even if it's a hybrid member, sometimes using a bot and sometimes writing manually, everybody should be aware that this kind of user is not entirely human and they repeatedly create posts with content generated by AIs. Of course, this only applies for users that repeatdely do that. Example: 03) AI Comment - after some reports, mod is allowed to classify this kind of comment as AI generated. As a result of it, the comment is still visible and available, but you need to click to view it. So, if you want to read a generic answer, go ahead, but most people don't want to read this kind of stuff and we wouldn't even need to quickly scrow down as we are doing right now. My additional suggestion: a comment flagged as AI generated doesn't count for your post counter - that would discourage them. The only issue I can see about it is giving more work for our busy mods. Thinking about it, maybe Jr.Executive members could also be allowed to view those reports and take action, raising the amount of people with rights to do that (except for the second suggestion ,that would require a mod action). But if our mods can handle that, this can be ignored.

title: My Journey - 2020-2025 as a Twitch SMM Panel Owner- Puresmm.com
author: PURESMM
content: I've been lurking and contributing here for a while (shoutout to my Jr. VIP status!), and I figured it's time to share my journey in the SMM world. Started back in 2020, and it's been a wild ride building PureSMM.com into what it is today. If you're into SMM panels, reselling, or just grinding your own IM projects, hopefully my story and learnings give you some value. I'll keep it real no fluff, just the highs, lows, and key insights that helped me scale. The Humble Beginnings (2020-2021)​It all kicked off during the pandemic when I was tinkering with my own accounts for Twitch, Kick, and Trovo. I developed custom bots and services to boost viewers, followers, and chatters nothing fancy, just real, active engagement starting at dirt-cheap prices like $0.01 per viewer. Started serving a handful of users manually, handling everything myself. Word spread through small Discord groups and forums, and before I knew it, demand grew. That's when I launched PureSMM.com as a full panel. No big funding or team; just me coding late nights turning my scripts into a user-friendly dashboard. Fast forward: We've hit 1.3 Million+ total orders and 17k+ registered users and counting. Key Learning #1: Give Users Total Control—They'll Love You for It​Early on, I realized customers hate waiting on support tickets. They want instant control post-purchase. So, I built advanced features right into the panel: Pause/resume viewers anytime with one click. Customize intervals, chat messages, poll participation, and even raid followups. Easy API integration (REST, works with PerfectPanel) for seamless automation. Real-time controls like speed settings (fast, normal, slow) and live viewer dashboards. This was a game-changer. Users (and resellers) kept coming back because they could manage everything themselves—no middleman. Sellers especially dig it since they avoid chatting with support; it's all self-serve. Pro tip: If you're building a panel, prioritize user empowerment—it builds loyalty and reduces your overhead. Key Learning #2: Navigating the Big Players and Price Wars​The SMM market is cutthroat. Big-name panel owners only want to collaborate if you offer custom pricing that makes sense for them. I followed up relentlessly multiple emails, Skypes, you name it and locked in deals by giving competitive rates without undercutting my quality. But here's the real eye-opener: This niche is hyper price-conscious. People will jump ship for 0.1 cent cheaper elsewhere. I held my ground. Didn't slash prices; instead, focused on what sets PureSMM apart real, active followers/viewers (no bots, all legit accounts with profiles), instant delivery, and 24/7 support. Quality and speed win long-term. Sure, I lost some bargain-hunters, but it attracted serious streamers aiming for Twitch Affiliate/Partner status as well as panel owners. We've helped 100+ hit Affiliate and 50+ get Partner verified! Key Learning #3: Focus on What You Own—Don't Just Resell​Tempted to expand by reselling every service under the sun? I learned the hard way: It keeps you as "just a reseller" with limited growth. I stuck to what I built and can scale myself specializing in Twitch, Kick, and Trovo viewers, followers, chatters, and custom packages (weekly/monthly for steady growth). Insight for fellow panel owners: The big guys prefer working directly with developers like me. It cuts out layers, ensures reliability, and opens doors to custom integrations. Reselling someone else's stuff? You're always at their mercy. Issues : Lots of users dispute payments saying they didn't receive it. I have no option other than to refund. I focus on clients who make money so they are happy with the service and its a WIN WIN. Platform rules change time to time so it requires pulling off some all nighters fixing the issues. What's Next?​I'll keep sharing updates in this thread more learnings, challenges (like dealing with platform TOS changes), and wins.

title: Why most link builders fail (and it's not what you think)
author: Faisal Mirza
content: People think link builders fail because they can't find sites or write good content. Nope. They fail because they give up too early. Most people pitch a site once, get rejected, move on forever. Real link builders pitch, get rejected, wait 2 weeks, pitch again with a different angle. And again. And again. I got placements on sites that rejected me 3+ times. Persistence > perfection. How many times do you follow up before marking a prospect dead?

title: What type of Facebook content brings the most paying clients?
author: Lummypro
content: Not gonna lie, I’ve been experimenting with different content types on my pages recently, and some stuff actually converts better than I expected. Here’s what I’ve noticed works well: Videos: Grab attention fast. People watch, comment, and sometimes share if it hits the right spot. Stories: Great for behind-the-scenes or quick updates. Not huge on traffic, but builds trust over time. Standard posts: Still effective if they’re relatable or meaningful, otherwise people scroll past. Live streams: A bit tricky, but engagement is crazy high when done right. People ask questions and interact in real time. Polls or interactive posts: Surprisingly good at getting people talking and participating without feeling pushy. From my experience, mixing things up works best. One week a video, next week a post or poll, sprinkle in a live now and then. Keeps your audience curious and engaged. How about you guys, which content types actually bring paying clients for your pages? Or do you find it’s more about keeping a consistent presence overall?

title: How AI Is Enhancing Video Quality for Affiliate Marketers.
author: SpyLead
content: By 2025, video quality has become a critical factor in digital performance. Whether you're building brand awareness or running performance-driven campaigns, sharp and engaging visuals can significantly impact how audiences respond. The evolution of neural networks has fundamentally reshaped video production workflows, enabling creators to improve image clarity, modernize outdated footage, and even automate complex editing processes — all through artificial intelligence. How Neural Networks Are Changing Video Editing​Modern neural networks are no longer experimental tools — they are practical, production-ready solutions used across industries. These systems can identify frame-level imperfections and apply precise enhancements without human intervention. Among the most impactful capabilities: Upscaling resolution and restoring lost detail Increasing frame rates to improve motion fluidity Removing compression noise and visual artifacts Colorizing black-and-white footage Stabilizing handheld or shaky recordings Filling in missing or corrupted frames Automatically editing and sequencing clips Recognizing and erasing unwanted objects Such features are made possible through deep learning models trained on massive datasets of visual media. These models predict how clean, high-quality footage should appear — even when the original source is blurry or degraded — and apply context-aware corrections with impressive realism. Top AI Tools for Upscaling and Sharpening Video​Several AI-based platforms now stand out for their ability to transform raw or low-quality footage into production-ready content. These tools are especially valuable for affiliate marketers working with limited resources or time. Topaz Video Enhance AI​ One of the most widely adopted tools in professional video enhancement, Topaz combines multiple specialized models to handle different types of visual degradation: Artemis focuses on general upscaling, with support for resolutions up to 8K Proteus targets facial detail recovery Apollo is optimized for reducing noise and compression damage Its output is often indistinguishable from native HD or 4K video, making it a strong choice for campaigns where visual quality drives credibility. DAIN (Depth-Aware Frame Interpolation)​ DAIN excels at generating intermediate frames to increase FPS (frames per second). This makes motion smoother and more natural — ideal for: Low-frame-rate archival videos Slow-motion edits Animation sequences By analyzing the depth of each frame, DAIN predicts how objects should move between moments, helping restore flow to otherwise choppy footage. DeepFaceLab​ Originally created for facial replacement, DeepFaceLab has also proven effective for improving facial clarity in video. It can: Sharpen facial features Restore subtle expressions Adjust shadows and lighting Improve skin textures Though it demands significant processing power, it's particularly valuable for interview-style or face-focused content. RunwayML​ Runway offers a suite of generative AI tools built for video creators. It supports: Style transfers and artistic transformations Object removal and background editing Text-to-video generation Realistic texture synthesis The platform is especially useful for creating high-impact, scroll-stopping visual content without needing a large creative team. Video2X​ An open-source solution geared toward customization and batch processing. It supports several enhancement models: Waifu2X — optimized for animated content SRMD — well-suited for realistic scenes RealSR — delivers strong results in architectural or nature footage Video2X allows creators to fine-tune processing settings for greater control over results, particularly when working with larger libraries of content. AVCLabs Video Enhancer AI​ This all-in-one solution is designed for fast, accessible enhancement without compromising on quality. Features include: Upscaling to 4K and 8K Black-and-white colorization Stabilization of shaky footage Noise reduction and visual cleanup Thanks to its intuitive interface, AVCLabs is well-suited for marketers and content teams with limited post-production experience. How to Use AI to Restore or Improve Low-Quality Footage​ Improving video with neural networks typically involves a multi-step process. To achieve the best results: Start by analyzing the source: Is the problem resolution, jitter, artifacts, or color? Choose the most appropriate tool based on the issue Apply basic pre-processing if needed — such as cropping or stabilizing Run tests on short clips to find optimal settings Process the full video using the chosen model Apply finishing touches, such as color grading or visual effects In many cases, combining several tools yields better outcomes. For example, stabilize the footage first, remove digital noise second, and upscale last. This approach minimizes the chance of amplifying existing flaws. title: Why your Meta and Shopify sales data never match (and how to fix it for high-ticket items)
author: falinyuan
content: Attribution is a total nightmare for most of us. You see conversions happening on Facebook, Google, or with your affiliates, but then you check the Shopify "Conversion Details" and the path is either empty or totally broken. This is especially brutal if you’re selling high-ticket items like power stations, ebikes, or robotic mowers in that $1,000 to $2,000 range. The customer journey for these products is long. People don't just "click and buy." They see you on FB, search you on Google, read reviews on Reddit, and maybe hit a blogger’s link before finally pulling the trigger. Here is exactly why the data is a mess and how to actually deal with it. 1. The gap in how they count Meta uses a 7-day click and 1-day view attribution. It tracks when someone interacts with an ad and converts later. Shopify, on the other hand, records the order exactly when it’s created. For high-ticket stuff, a customer might see your FB ad on Monday, check a YouTube review on Wednesday, and finally buy on Friday. FB will claim that sale, but Shopify’s "Last Click" logic might give it to the YouTube link or just mark it as direct. Plus, FB has a 1-2 day reporting delay while Shopify is real-time. This alone makes your daily reports look like a disaster. 2. Cross-device and Cookie tracking Facebook is great at cross-device tracking because people stay logged into the app. They can link a mobile click to a desktop purchase. Shopify is different. It relies on "Sessions." If a customer clicks your ad on their phone but finishes the purchase on their work laptop, Shopify usually sees two completely different people. If they clear their cookies or use incognito mode, Shopify is basically blind. This is where most of that data discrepancy comes from. So, how do we fix this? First, you need Hybrid Tracking (Pixel + CAPI). Just having the Pixel on your site isn't enough anymore. You have to set up the Conversion API (CAPI) to send data directly from your server. Make sure you’re tracking the full funnel: View Content, Add to Cart, Initiate Checkout, and Purchase. This helps recover a lot of the data lost to browser privacy settings. Second, stop being lazy with UTMs. Every single ad, influencer link, or affiliate post needs a clean UTM. Don’t just wing it. Use the Google Campaign URL Builder to keep your Source, Medium, and Campaign names consistent. If you’re working with a lot of different channels, don't do them one by one. We usually build a bulk UTM generator in Excel. It’s way faster and ensures the whole team is using the exact same tags so Shopify can actually capture the source. Third, do a weekly data audit. Pick a day every week to manually sync your Meta ads manager with your Shopify "Paid Orders." Make sure the time zones match up first. If you see a massive gap that wasn't there before, it’s a red flag that your tracking code might be broken or a specific channel is cannibalizing your attribution.

title: Why most FB ad accounts die (and it’s not always bad luck)
author: Lanfire Agency
content: Lately I see a lot of people saying “FB is killing everything” — accounts die, BMs get restricted, spend can’t scale From my side, after running and maintaining ad accounts through multiple review waves, one thing became clear: Most accounts don’t die because of bad luck. They die because of bad usage. That said — let’s be honest — sometimes Facebook is just dumb. They wipe everything, good or bad, with no logic at all. It happens. But over time, you still see a pattern. An account is not something you just plug in and push spend. It needs: proper warm-up controlled spend rhythm knowing when to pause instead of forcing I’ve seen accounts survive 2–3 major cleanups with stable daily spend, while others die in days. At this stage, stability matters more than price. Not selling anything here. Just sharing experience. title: Why bing ads account blocked
author: georgemart
content: If your Bing Ads account has been suspended, it means that Microsoft Advertising (formerly known as Bing Ads) has determined that your account or ads have violated their advertising policies. Bing Ads, similar to other advertising platforms, has guidelines in place to ensure the quality and appropriateness of ads displayed on their network. Here are some possible reasons for Bing Ads account suspensions: Policy Violations: Bing Ads has specific policies regarding ad content, landing pages, user experience, and other aspects of advertising. If your account or ads violate any of these policies, it can lead to a suspension. Examples of policy violations include promoting prohibited products, using misleading claims, or displaying inappropriate content.

title: Keyword Research in 2026 - Has Your Process Changed? (Agency Work vs Your Own Sites)
author: ads studio
content: Genuinely curious how people here approach this differently depending on who you're doing it for. I manage SEO for client sites AND run my own niche/affiliate projects on the side and honestly, my keyword research process looks completely different depending on which hat I'm wearing. For clients, I'm pulled toward safer, data-heavy approaches. Justifying every decision with volume numbers and KD scores because that's what they understand. For my own sites, I'm way more experimental chasing intent gaps, weak SERP signals, stuff I'd never pitch in a client deck. It got me thinking: does the tool you use actually change based on the context? A few things I'm genuinely curious about from people doing both: 1. Do you use the same keyword research tool for client work and your own sites? Or do you keep them separate — paid/professional for clients, scrappier methods for yourself? 2. Has AI changed how you do initial keyword research? Not talking about replacing Ahrefs — but are you using ChatGPT, Perplexity, or anything else to speed up the ideation phase before you even open a KW tool? 3. What's one keyword research method you swear by that most people here probably aren't doing? Could be a tool, a process, a way of reading SERPs — anything. 4. Free vs paid — where's your honest line? At what point did paid tools actually move the needle for you? Or are you still winning with free? I'll share what's working for me after I see some responses. Want to hear real workflows, not just tool names.

title: Site In Escrow  - Who Is Right Here?
